does: This paper proposes representation-aware advantage estimation for reinforcement learning from human feedback, introducing Graph-based Advantage Estimation (GraphAE) that leverages hidden representations from reward models rather than relying solely on scalar reward outputs. The method treats sampled response groups as graphs where nodes represent responses and edges encode similarity in the reward model's hidden space, then computes advantages through graph propagation so each sample incorporates contextual information from neighboring responses. GraphAE is designed as a lightweight module that integrates into existing group-based RL algorithms like GRPO, GSPO, and RLOO, and experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in performance.
connects: This work builds on the RLHF paradigm that has become central to training large language models, extending beyond the standard approach of using only scalar reward signals from trained reward models. It responds to the recognized limitation that scalar rewards are noisy and coarse-grained, building on prior observations that neural networks learn rich representations encoding nuanced information. The paper connects to graph neural network methods and graph-based learning approaches, adapting these techniques to leverage the relational structure between responses within sampled groups, while also building on recent advances in group-based reinforcement learning algorithms that batch responses for efficiency.
enables: This work opens directions for deeper integration of representation learning and preference modeling in RLHF systems. It suggests future research could extract richer supervisory signals from intermediate model layers, potentially leading to more robust reward modeling that better captures complex human preferences. The approach enables development of more sample-efficient RL algorithms by better utilizing information already present in trained reward models, and points toward methods that leverage structural relationships between samples during training. This could inform future work on reward model design, multi-faceted preference modeling, and more sophisticated use of model representations in reinforcement learning from human feedback broadly.
